channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Dark energy flips its sign, but the Hubble tension refuses to budge - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Dark energy flips its sign, but the Hubble tension refuses to budge  Phys.org
  2. Astronomers Use a Neutron Star Merger to Measure Cosmic Expansion  Universe Today
  3. Dark Energy Survey Data Hints At Non-Constant Expansion  Quantum Zeitgeist
  4. Yes, the Universe’s Expansion Is Still Accelerating, Researchers Say  The Japan News

item.description:

item.title: F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry - The New York Times
item.description:
1. F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry  The New York Times

  3. FCC Approves Reflect Orbital's Space Mirror Satellite That Astronomers Hate  PCMag
  4. The U.S. just approved a giant space mirror to test ‘sunlight on demand.’ Low Earth orbit is getting weird  The Conversation
  5. In 2026, the FCC authorized radio operations for Reflect Orbital’s first 18-metre space mirror, a single demonstration for a proposed 50,000-satellite system that one astronomy model says could raise the natural night-sky background by 200% to 300%  Space Daily

item.title: On July 7, a small Miami company called City Labs put a tritium-powered battery the size of a pencil eraser into orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 — its softball-sized BOHR CubeSat becoming the first commercial spacecraft ever cleared to carry a nuclear power - Space Daily
item.description:
1. On July 7, a small Miami company called City Labs put a tritium-powered battery the size of a pencil eraser into orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 — its softball-sized BOHR CubeSat becoming the first commercial spacecraft ever cleared to carry a nuclear power  Space Daily
  2. Transporter-17 Mission  SpaceX
  3. Miami-based City Labs achieves a first for commercial nuclear power in space  Ars Technica
  4. SpaceX just launched the 1st-ever nuclear-powered commercial satellite  Space
  5. In July 2026, a Miami startup called City Labs sent a softball-sized CubeSat carrying a tritium battery into orbit on SpaceX's Transporter-17 rideshare, becoming the first commercially built nuclear-powered satellite ever launched and the first mission ever cleare  Space Daily

  4. Scientists create a 'mini universe' that tells time without any clock | Scientists refer to this as 'entropic time' | Inshorts  Inshorts
  5. What if time isn't real? Physicists built a tiny quantum universe with 24,000 ultracold atoms and found a shocking truth: Time could create itself  The Times of India

  3. 90,000-year-old Homo sapiens jaw wound points to possible violence in early human groups  Archaeology News Online Magazine
  4. Human in Israel Smashed in the Face With a Rock 100,000 Years Ago  Haaretz
  5. 100,000 years ago, one of the earliest Homo sapiens outside Africa was stabbed in the face, analysis finds  Live Science

item.title: An exoplanet 124 light-years away may harbour a global ocean beneath a hydrogen-rich atmosphere. Webb has produced two tentative hints of dimethyl sulfide—a gas produced mainly by marine microorganisms on Earth—but independent analyses dispute - Space Daily
item.description:
1. An exoplanet 124 light-years away may harbour a global ocean beneath a hydrogen-rich atmosphere. Webb has produced two tentative hints of dimethyl sulfide—a gas produced mainly by marine microorganisms on Earth—but independent analyses dispute  Space Daily
  2. Scientists have found 99.7% chance of life in this planet and no, it's not Mars  The Times of India

item.title: Astronomers believe the Milky Way may contain billions, perhaps even trillions, of rogue planets drifting without close stars — and a 2026 study found one 9,800 light-years away that was likely born in a planetary system before being thrown into the dark - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Astronomers believe the Milky Way may contain billions, perhaps even trillions, of rogue planets drifting without close stars — and a 2026 study found one 9,800 light-years away that was likely born in a planetary system before being thrown into the dark  Space Daily
  2. We tend to assume life needs a star to begin, but a 2025 study found that some moons carried into deep space by planets expelled during supernova explosions could preserve subsurface oceans for billions of years — heated not by sunlight, but by the repeat  Space Daily

item.title: NASA is keeping Voyager 1 alive by switching off instruments and heaters one by one—but it cannot let the spacecraft become so cold that its fuel lines freeze. If those lines fail, Voyager could lose the thrusters that keep its antenna aimed at Earth, permanently - Space Daily
item.description:
1. NASA is keeping Voyager 1 alive by switching off instruments and heaters one by one—but it cannot let the spacecraft become so cold that its fuel lines freeze. If those lines fail, Voyager could lose the thrusters that keep its antenna aimed at Earth, permanently  Space Daily
  2. Voyager Spacecraft: The Ultimate Power Management Challenge?  EE Times
  3. The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery  Space Daily
  4. 50 Years Later, NASA's Voyager Probe Is Reaching A Big Milestone For Space Travel  bgr.com
  5. NASA’s Voyager 1 left Earth in 1977; now its signal takes over 22 hours to reach us and it is still sending data  The Times of India

item.title: In June 2026, the James Webb telescope read the dawn and dusk skies of WASP-121b separately and found one side hotter and stripped of water, the other cooler and possibly cloudy, on a world where it can rain liquid rock - Space Daily
item.description: In June 2026, the James Webb telescope read the dawn and dusk skies of WASP-121b separately and found one side hotter and stripped of water, the other cooler and possibly cloudy, on a world where it can rain liquid rock  Space Daily

item.title: We assumed water had to be delivered to planets by comets or asteroids — but a 2025 Nature study found that sub-Neptunes, the most common type of planet in the galaxy, can forge their own oceans by reacting hydrogen atmospheres with molten rock deep - Space Daily

item.title: Saturn's rings are so young that dinosaurs would have looked up at a ringless planet, and measurements from the Cassini probe suggest they are being pulled into the planet fast enough to disappear entirely - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Saturn's rings are so young that dinosaurs would have looked up at a ringless planet, and measurements from the Cassini probe suggest they are being pulled into the planet fast enough to disappear entirely  Space Daily
  2. Saturn’s rings may be far younger than the planet itself — Cassini-era estimates have placed their age anywhere from about 10 million to a few hundred million years, meaning the rings we see today may not have existed for most of the age of dinosaurs.  Space Daily

item.title: A supermassive black hole called RBH-1, weighing at least ten million Suns, appears to have been flung from its galaxy and is now racing through space at nearly 1,000 kilometres per second, leaving a 200,000-light-year wake of newborn stars - Space Daily

1. Astronomers may have heard the 1st 'whispers' of ghost particles created by supernova explosions  Space
  2. Cosmic neutrino 'whispers' may surface in 5,000-day Super-Kamiokande signal  Phys.org
  3. First indication of diffuse supernova neutrino background emerges from Super-Kamiokande  The Watchers - Watching the world evolve and transform

item.title: Scientists say 'super-Earth' located 25 light-years away from us may be a top place to look for life - Yahoo
item.description:
1. Scientists say 'super-Earth' located 25 light-years away from us may be a top place to look for life  Yahoo
  2. Alien life on nearby exoplanet more likely than previously thought  The Jerusalem Post
  3. In November 2025, UC Irvine astronomers announced a rocky super-Earth called GJ 251 c orbiting in a habitable zone less than 20 light-years away — close enough that the next generation of ground-based telescopes may actually photograph it directly  Space Daily
  4. Nearby "Super Earth" Could Host Life After All  Universe Today
  5. This nearby exoplanet reignites hope: new signs of possible alien life detected  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
